title: Crusty rolls
subtitle: Small French-style rolls with a shattering crust
excerpt: Crusty rolls are a lean, high-hydration dough baked with steam to produce a shell-like crust that shatters when you press it and a light, open crumb underneath. The key is the steam in the first 10 minutes of baking, which keeps the surface supple long enough for the roll to spring up before the crust sets.
sourceNotes: Crisp-crusted small rolls (petit pain, kaisersemmel) appear across European baking traditions. The key technique of using steam to develop crust is documented in French professional baking texts. Elizabeth David, English Bread and Yeast Cookery (1977), discusses the role of steam in achieving a crisp crust on small breads.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze when fully cool. Refresh from frozen in a 200°C oven for 8 to 10 minutes; the crust revives almost completely.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape and retard overnight in the fridge; bake from cold, adding 3 to 4 minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; remove steam source after 10 minutes and leave door ajar for 5 minutes if possible
baking.steamMethod: water tray on oven floor for the first 10 minutes

content.text: Crusty rolls are a lean, slightly wet dough baked hard and fast with steam. The steam keeps the surface pliable while the inside springs up, then the crust sets in the dry heat and emerges thin, crisp, and shattering. A 70% hydration is wetter than a standard tin-loaf dough; the rolls will be slightly sticky to handle but the crumb will be more open and light.

Combine flour, yeast, and salt. Add the water and mix to a shaggy dough. Knead for 10 minutes by hand; the dough will be softer and stickier than a tin-loaf dough throughout. Resist flouring the surface. At 70% hydration the dough clears the bowl but clings slightly to the hands; this is correct. Use a bench scraper to turn and fold the dough if needed.
Cover and leave at room temperature for 75 minutes. The dough should more than double.

With floured hands, turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface and cut into 8 equal pieces. Shape each piece gently into a round, using cupped hands and a light touch; overworking a wet dough knocks out the structure. Place on a floured baking tray and cover. Prove for 45 minutes.

Heat the oven to 230°C fan with a roasting tin on the bottom shelf. Just before loading the rolls, pour a cup of water into the hot roasting tin to generate steam. Score each roll with a single slash or cross. Bake for 20 minutes. After 10 minutes, remove the steam source and leave the oven door ajar for 30 seconds to let it dry out. The rolls are done when they are deep gold, shattering-crisp on the surface, and sound hollow when tapped underneath.
